backstory: My involvement centered around supporting and growing the account, helping the customer understand how solar lighting could improve visibility, safety, and usability along the trail. Projects like this are not just about selling lights. They are about understanding how people move through public spaces, how cities and agencies think about maintenance, and how infrastructure decisions affect the daily experience of residents, visitors, cyclists, and pedestrians.

name: Bedford Public Library
description: This project involved a cooperative contract purchase for solar lighting around an adjacent open area at a public library. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and usability in a community space conne
backstory:
This project involved a cooperative contract purchase for solar lighting around an adjacent open area at a public library. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and usability in a community space connected to the library.

My role was connected to supporting the purchasing path and helping position solar lighting as a practical solution through an existing cooperative contract. That made the process easier for the customer and gave them a clear way to move forward without having to build a new procurement from scratch.

For me, this project represents experience with cooperative contract purchasing, public library infrastructure, and solar lighting applications that improve community spaces. It showed how understanding the right purchasing vehicle can help turn a local need into a completed project.

name: Tatton Park Drive
description: This project involved solar sign lighting for a neighborhood, focused on improving visibility and presentation at the community entrance or key signage area. The opportunity showed how a simple solar lighting solution ca
backstory:
This project involved solar sign lighting for a neighborhood, focused on improving visibility and presentation at the community entrance or key signage area. The opportunity showed how a simple solar lighting solution can make a neighborhood feel more visible, welcoming, and cared for without requiring a traditional electrical connection.

My role was connected to supporting the sales process and helping position solar lighting as a practical fit for the customer’s needs. For neighborhood signage, lighting is not just about illumination — it affects curb appeal, safety, identity, and how the community presents itself after dark.

For me, this project represents experience with smaller-scale solar lighting applications that still create real value. It showed how renewable lighting can support residential communities, improve appearance, and solve a visibility need in a clean and practical way.

backstory:
This project involved solar lighting for the West Ashley Greenway in Charleston, South Carolina. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and usability along a public greenway using solar-powered lighting infrastructure.

My role was connected to supporting the sales process and helping position solar lighting as a practical fit for the greenway environment. For public trails and greenways, lighting plays an important role in how people experience the space, especially during early morning, evening, or lower-light conditions.

For me, this project represents experience with municipal parks and recreation infrastructure, solar trail lighting, and public-space improvements. It showed how solar technology can support safer and more usable greenways without requiring a full traditional electrical buildout.

description: This project involved solar parking lot lighting for a veterans monument area in Oaklyn, New Jersey. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and access around a meaningful public space dedicated to honor
backstory:
This project involved solar parking lot lighting for a veterans monument area in Oaklyn, New Jersey. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and access around a meaningful public space dedicated to honoring veterans.

My role was connected to supporting the sales process and helping position solar lighting as a practical solution for the site. For a location like a veterans monument, lighting carries more weight than just function. It helps make the space feel cared for, visible, and respectful, especially during evening hours or public gatherings.

For me, this project represents experience with municipal public-space lighting and community-focused infrastructure. It showed how solar lighting can support safety, accessibility, and civic pride in places that hold real meaning for the community.

name: U.S. Department of Homeland Security
description: This project involved a large multi-unit deployment of solar generators across multiple locations for the U.S. Department of Homeland Security. The opportunity required more than a basic product sale because the solution
backstory:
This project involved a large multi-unit deployment of solar generators across multiple locations for the U.S. Department of Homeland Security. The opportunity required more than a basic product sale because the solution needed to support a federal agency with real operational needs across different sites.

My role was connected to supporting the bid process, positioning the solar generator solution, and helping move a large government opportunity from procurement into deployment. Projects like this require an understanding of technical requirements, federal purchasing, logistics, and the importance of reliable off-grid power in the field.

For me, this project represents experience with federal government sales, multi-location deployment, and renewable power solutions at scale. It showed my ability to support complex public-sector opportunities where capability, trust, and execution all matter.

name: Louisiana State University in Shreveport
description: This project involved a solar table that I sold through a cold RFP opportunity, installed, and captured on video. It was a full-circle project that started with finding and responding to the opportunity, then continued t
backstory:
This project involved a solar table that I sold through a cold RFP opportunity, installed, and captured on video. It was a full-circle project that started with finding and responding to the opportunity, then continued through delivery, installation, and final presentation.

My role covered more than just the sales side. I helped move the opportunity forward through the RFP process, supported the installation, and created video content to document the finished product in use. The solar table gave the customer a visible public amenity that combined outdoor seating, device charging, and renewable energy in one practical solution.

For me, this project represents the full cycle of opportunity development, public-sector sales, hands-on execution, and storytelling. It showed my ability to find the opportunity, win the work, help bring the product to life, and create content that showed the value after installation.

name: Tufts University
description: This project involved solar tables for a university, sold through a distributor relationship on an inherited account. The opportunity showed the importance of stepping into an existing relationship, understanding the his
backstory:
This project involved solar tables for a university, sold through a distributor relationship on an inherited account. The opportunity showed the importance of stepping into an existing relationship, understanding the history, and continuing to support the account in a way that kept the project moving forward.

My role was connected to managing the sales process through the distributor, supporting the account, and helping position the solar tables as a strong fit for a campus environment. For a university, these tables were more than outdoor furniture — they created a useful public amenity where students, staff, and visitors could gather, charge devices, and experience renewable energy in a practical way.

For me, this project represents experience with distributor sales, inherited account management, and solar-powered campus infrastructure. It showed my ability to take over an existing relationship, support the channel partner, and help turn the opportunity into a completed project.

name: Louisiana State Police Headquarters
description: This project involved custom solar generator solutions for Louisiana State Police Headquarters in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. The opportunity came through a cold RFP, meaning there was no warm relationship driving the deal a
backstory:
This project involved custom solar generator solutions for Louisiana State Police Headquarters in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. The opportunity came through a cold RFP, meaning there was no warm relationship driving the deal at the beginning. It had to be won through the strength of the response, the fit of the solution, and the ability to show that the product could meet the customer’s needs.

My role was connected to managing the RFP opportunity, positioning the solar generator solution, and helping turn a formal bid into a real project win. The project showed the value of understanding technical requirements, public-sector procurement, and how to present renewable power equipment in a way that makes sense for a government/public safety customer.

What made this project stronger was that the relationship did not stop after the first purchase. After the initial RFP win, the customer came back for additional purchases, which showed trust in the product, the process, and the support behind it.

For me, this project represents a cold RFP win that turned into repeat business. It showed my ability to compete through procurement, win on capability, and help create enough confidence for the customer to return for more.

name: Cary
description: This project started as a North Carolina pilot for solar bus stop lighting and grew into a multi-unit deployment. The opportunity showed how a small initial installation can prove the value of a solution and create the c
backstory:
This project started as a North Carolina pilot for solar bus stop lighting and grew into a multi-unit deployment. The opportunity showed how a small initial installation can prove the value of a solution and create the confidence needed for a larger rollout.

My role was connected to supporting the sales and account process, helping move the project from an initial test into a broader deployment. With bus stop lighting, the value is easy to understand once the product is in the field. Better lighting improves visibility, rider comfort, and the overall perception of safety at transit stops.

For me, this project represents experience turning pilots into expanded opportunities. It showed how follow-through, customer support, and real-world product performance can move a project from “let’s test it” to “let’s deploy more.”

name: Martin County
description: This project involved solar bus stop lighting for Martin County, Florida. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and rider comfort at transit stops through a practical solar-powered lighting solution.
backstory:
This project involved solar bus stop lighting for Martin County, Florida. The opportunity focused on improving visibility, safety, and rider comfort at transit stops through a practical solar-powered lighting solution.

My role was connected to supporting the sales process, helping position the Transit Pole System as a strong fit for the county’s transportation needs, and moving the opportunity forward through a direct sale. For bus stop environments, lighting is not just about equipment — it affects how riders feel while waiting, how visible stops are after dark, and how communities experience public transportation.

For me, this project represents experience with county-level transit infrastructure, solar bus stop lighting, and practical public-sector solutions. It showed how solar technology can help agencies improve rider safety and stop visibility without needing a full traditional electrical buildout.

name: oc waste and recycling

name: Robert C. Porter Family Park
description: This project involved a solar lighting solution for Robert C. Porter Family Park in Champaign, Illinois. The opportunity required both technical understanding and teamwork because the manufacturer rep involved had never
backstory:
This project involved a solar lighting solution for Robert C. Porter Family Park in Champaign, Illinois. The opportunity required both technical understanding and teamwork because the manufacturer rep involved had never sold solar lighting before.

My role was to guide the rep through the solar side of the opportunity, lead the meeting with the agency, and handle the internal project work needed to position the solution correctly. That included helping connect the customer’s park lighting needs to the right solar product and supporting the process that led to the system being specified in the bid.

The project ultimately moved forward as a direct purchase, showing how early coordination, product knowledge, and strong communication can shape an opportunity before it reaches procurement.

For me, this project represents the value of teamwork and technical sales leadership. It showed my ability to support a rep, lead a customer conversation, manage the behind-the-scenes work, and help turn a park lighting need into a solar project win.

name: Snead State Community College
description: This project involved a solar table opportunity for Snead State Community College in Boaz, Alabama. The RFP appeared to be positioned around another specified manufacturer, but I identified a path to compete and submitte
backstory:
This project involved a solar table opportunity for Snead State Community College in Boaz, Alabama. The RFP appeared to be positioned around another specified manufacturer, but I identified a path to compete and submitted a strong response that ultimately won the bid.

My role was connected to reviewing the RFP, understanding the customer’s requirements, positioning our solution correctly, and showing that we could meet the need even without being the originally specified manufacturer. Because this was a cold RFP win, the project had to be earned through the quality of the response, product fit, and confidence in our ability to deliver.

For me, this project represents the ability to compete when the opportunity is not originally built for you. It showed my experience with higher education procurement, technical positioning, and turning a difficult bid into a real win.

name: Valparaiso City Park Department
description: This parks project was a strong example of teamwork between a distributor and manufacturer support. The distributor opened the door and set the meeting, and my role was to lead the customer conversation, explain the solu
backstory:
This parks project was a strong example of teamwork between a distributor and manufacturer support. The distributor opened the door and set the meeting, and my role was to lead the customer conversation, explain the solution, and help position the project in a way that made sense for the agency’s needs.

During the meeting, I helped connect the customer’s park infrastructure goals to the right solar solution, answering questions and showing how the product could improve visibility, safety, and usability in the space. The project moved forward because the opportunity was handled as a team — the distributor brought the relationship, and I supported the technical and sales side needed to close it.

For me, this project represents the value of partnership selling. It showed my ability to step into a customer-facing meeting, lead with product knowledge, support a channel partner, and help turn a parks opportunity into a sold project.

name: Downtown Greenway Morehead Park Trailhead Parking
description: This project involved solar lighting for the Greensboro Downtown Greenway at the Morehead Park Trailhead area. As a hometown project, this one carried a different kind of meaning because it connected my professional work
backstory:
This project involved solar lighting for the Greensboro Downtown Greenway at the Morehead Park Trailhead area. As a hometown project, this one carried a different kind of meaning because it connected my professional work in solar infrastructure with my civic involvement in Greensboro.

My role was connected to helping secure the specification and supporting the bid opportunity. Through relationships and visibility built from my Community Sustainability Committee work, I was able to help connect the need for sustainable public-space lighting with a real solution that fit the city’s goals.

The project mattered because greenways are more than trails. They are public spaces where safety, visibility, walkability, and community pride all come together. Solar lighting gave the city a way to improve the area while supporting sustainability and reducing the need for traditional electrical infrastructure.

For me, this project represents a hometown win. It showed how civic involvement, relationship-building, technical understanding, and sales execution can come together to create visible impact in the community I live in and care about.

name: Yadkin River Park
description: This project involved solar lighting and solar bollards for Yadkin River Park in Linwood, North Carolina. The relationship started at a national trade show and grew into one of my first real North Carolina customer accou
backstory:
This project involved solar lighting and solar bollards for Yadkin River Park in Linwood, North Carolina. The relationship started at a national trade show and grew into one of my first real North Carolina customer accounts.

What began with dog park bollards developed over time into a broader solar lighting opportunity at the boat ramp, including lighting and camera-related infrastructure. The account grew because the customer saw the value of the solution, and because the relationship was supported beyond the first conversation.

My role was connected to building the account, understanding the park’s needs, and helping expand the solution as new opportunities came up. For parks and public spaces, lighting is not just about visibility — it supports safety, usability, and confidence for the people using the space.

For me, this project represents account growth, relationship-building, and one of my earliest meaningful wins in North Carolina. It showed how a trade show conversation can turn into a real customer relationship when you follow up, understand the need, and keep building trust over time.

name: Metropolitan Evansville Transit System (METS)
description: This project involved a solar bus shelter for Metropolitan Evansville Transit System in Evansville, Indiana. The opportunity came through a cold RFP, which meant the project had to be won through the strength of the bid
backstory:
This project involved a solar bus shelter for Metropolitan Evansville Transit System in Evansville, Indiana. The opportunity came through a cold RFP, which meant the project had to be won through the strength of the bid response, the fit of the solution, and the ability to show value without an existing relationship.

My role was connected to reviewing the requirements, positioning the solar shelter solution, and supporting the bid process from opportunity to award. For a transit agency, a solar bus shelter is more than a structure — it improves the rider experience by adding visibility, comfort, and a more reliable public-facing amenity.

For me, this project represents experience winning cold public-sector RFPs in the transit space. It showed my ability to compete through procurement, position solar infrastructure correctly, and turn an open bid opportunity into a real project win.

name: GoTriangle
description: This project involved solar bus stop lighting for GoTriangle in Durham, North Carolina. The opportunity started with specification work and relationship-building with the agency, which helped create the path for a larger
backstory:
This project involved solar bus stop lighting for GoTriangle in Durham, North Carolina. The opportunity started with specification work and relationship-building with the agency, which helped create the path for a larger deployment of solar-powered transit lighting.

My role was connected to helping position the Transit Pole System as the right solution for the agency’s needs, supporting the technical and sales process, and building trust around how solar lighting could improve bus stop visibility, rider comfort, and safety.

The project mattered because transit lighting has a direct impact on the rider experience. Better lighting can make stops feel more visible, more reliable, and more connected to the public transportation system.

For me, this project represents the value of early specification work, agency relationship-building, and long-term opportunity development. It showed how understanding the customer’s needs and staying engaged through the process can turn an initial project conversation into a large solar bus stop lighting deployment.

name: Kansas Alliance for Wetlands and Streams
description: This project involved working directly with the Kansas Alliance for Wetlands and Streams on custom solar solutions for a walking trail environment. The opportunity included solar bollards and a solar shelter with electri
backstory:
This project involved working directly with the Kansas Alliance for Wetlands and Streams on custom solar solutions for a walking trail environment. The opportunity included solar bollards and a solar shelter with electric charging, creating a more functional and accessible outdoor space for visitors.

My role was connected to understanding the agency’s needs, helping shape the right solar solution, and supporting the direct sales process. Because this was not a one-size-fits-all project, it required listening to how the space would be used and matching the products to the trail, visitor experience, and long-term outdoor environment.

For me, this project represents experience with custom solar infrastructure, trail-focused public amenities, and direct collaboration with an agency. It showed how solar technology can support conservation, recreation, and public access while giving people practical features like lighting, shelter, and charging in outdoor spaces.

name: Los Angeles Department of Public Works
description: The LA Shelters project was a major public-sector win that I managed from the beginning of the bid process through submission and award. My involvement started with identifying the opportunity, understanding the requirem
backstory:
The LA Shelters project was a major public-sector win that I managed from the beginning of the bid process through submission and award. My involvement started with identifying the opportunity, understanding the requirements, coordinating the response, and helping make sure the project was positioned correctly for the agency’s needs.

This project was important because transit shelters directly affect the rider experience. Lighting, visibility, safety, and comfort all play a role in how people view public transportation. By bringing solar-powered shelter solutions into the project, we were able to support a more sustainable and practical approach to improving transit infrastructure.

For me, the LA Shelters project represents my ability to manage a government bid from start to finish — from finding the opportunity, organizing the details, supporting the submission, and helping secure the award. It was not just a sales opportunity; it was a full public-sector project win.

name: Truist Point Papercast Demonstration
description: This project was a hands-on product demo where I took a solar light and e-screen into the field to create real-world visual examples for the customer. Instead of only explaining the product with cut sheets or spec docume
backstory:
This project was a hands-on product demo where I took a solar light and e-screen into the field to create real-world visual examples for the customer. Instead of only explaining the product with cut sheets or spec documents, I helped show how the system could actually look and function in a real environment.

I used demo photos and drone footage to capture the product from different angles, giving the customer a better understanding of placement, visibility, scale, and overall impact. This helped turn the product from a technical concept into something they could clearly picture in use.

For me, this project represents the creative side of sales and account support. Sometimes moving an opportunity forward requires more than a quote — it takes initiative, visuals, storytelling, and the ability to help a customer see the finished product before it is installed.

name: Palm Tran Central County Facility
description: The Solar Solutions for Palm Tran project was an example of how important procurement strategy can be in public-sector sales. My role was focused on working through the procurement process so we could better position our
backstory:
The Solar Solutions for Palm Tran project was an example of how important procurement strategy can be in public-sector sales. My role was focused on working through the procurement process so we could better position ourselves for the opportunity and make sure we had a fair chance to submit.

By staying engaged with the bid requirements and procurement timeline, I helped create the conditions needed for the project to move forward. That work resulted in the bid dates being extended, which gave us the additional time needed to prepare and submit a stronger response.

For me, this project represents more than just chasing a bid. It shows the importance of understanding procurement, asking the right questions, staying active in the process, and knowing how to create room for a company to compete. Sometimes the win starts before the proposal is even submitted.

name: Raleigh
description: This enhanced solar shelter project was a strong example of turning a custom procurement opportunity into a real win. The bid was not a simple off-the-shelf request. It required understanding what the customer wanted, ho
backstory:
This enhanced solar shelter project was a strong example of turning a custom procurement opportunity into a real win. The bid was not a simple off-the-shelf request. It required understanding what the customer wanted, how the shelter needed to function, and how to position our solution as the right fit.

We won the project based on a combination of local recommendation, proven ability, and confidence in our experience with solar-powered transit infrastructure. My role was tied to helping move the opportunity through the process, supporting the bid, and making sure the customer could clearly see why our solution made sense for their needs.

For me, this project represents the value of reputation, preparation, and knowing how to respond when a procurement is built around a specific need. It showed that custom opportunities can be won when the solution is positioned correctly and the customer trusts the team behind it.

name: Westchester County
description: This solar bus stop lighting project was an example of taking an opportunity that started as a pilot and helping move it through the formal bid process. Another rep had originally started the account relationship, but on
backstory:
This solar bus stop lighting project was an example of taking an opportunity that started as a pilot and helping move it through the formal bid process. Another rep had originally started the account relationship, but once the opportunity moved into procurement, I handled the bid work needed to keep us positioned and competitive.

The project focused on solar-powered lighting for bus stops, which is important because lighting can directly affect rider safety, visibility, and comfort. For transit agencies, these types of projects are not just about installing equipment — they are about improving the rider experience while finding practical infrastructure solutions that do not always require traditional electrical work.

For me, this project represents the ability to step into an existing opportunity, understand the history, manage the bid requirements, and help carry the project forward when it became a formal procurement. It showed how teamwork, follow-through, and bid management all play a role in turning a pilot into a real project opportunity.

name: NAWCAD Lakehurst
description: This project involved solar generators purchased through the GSA Schedule for a naval/air force customer. The opportunity showed the value of having government contract vehicles in place and being ready when a federal cu
backstory:
This project involved solar generators purchased through the GSA Schedule for a naval/air force customer. The opportunity showed the value of having government contract vehicles in place and being ready when a federal customer needed a clean, portable power solution.

My role was connected to supporting the sales and procurement process, helping position the solar generator solution through an approved purchasing path. Because the purchase was made through GSA, the project also reflected the importance of compliance, contract readiness, and understanding how federal buyers prefer to acquire equipment.

For me, this project represents experience with federal purchasing, renewable power solutions, and the ability to support opportunities where speed, trust, and contract access matter. It was a strong example of how the right contract vehicle can help turn government interest into an actual order.

name: Denver Department of Public Health & Environment
description: This project was a bid I won to deliver custom solar generators for air quality monitoring in Colorado. The opportunity required more than a standard product response because the generators had to support environmental m
backstory:
This project was a bid I won to deliver custom solar generators for air quality monitoring in Colorado. The opportunity required more than a standard product response because the generators had to support environmental monitoring equipment in the field.

My role was focused on managing the bid, understanding the customer’s need, and helping position a solar-powered solution that could reliably support air quality monitoring without depending on traditional power access. This type of project mattered because air quality data is only valuable when the equipment can stay powered, placed correctly, and operate consistently in real-world conditions.

For me, this project represents experience with custom renewable power solutions, environmental monitoring, and public-sector bid work. It showed my ability to take a specialized requirement, connect it to the right solar solution, and turn that opportunity into a win.

name: Florida
description: This project involved working with a contractor that already held an FDOT contract to deliver solar lighting for park-and-ride locations in Florida. My role was connected to supporting the opportunity, working through th
backstory:
This project involved working with a contractor that already held an FDOT contract to deliver solar lighting for park-and-ride locations in Florida. My role was connected to supporting the opportunity, working through the account and procurement path, and helping position solar lighting as a practical solution for transportation infrastructure.

The project mattered because park-and-rides are important connection points in a transit system. Lighting affects safety, visibility, and how comfortable people feel leaving their vehicles or waiting for transportation. Solar lighting made sense because it provided a way to improve these locations without relying completely on traditional electrical infrastructure.

For me, this project represents experience working through contractor-led public-sector opportunities, supporting FDOT-related infrastructure work, and helping deliver renewable lighting solutions in real transportation environments.

name: Hidalgo County

backstory:
This project involved a direct purchase of a smart solar crosswalk solution. The opportunity showed how solar-powered safety infrastructure can be used to improve visibility, pedestrian awareness, and public safety without requiring a full traditional electrical buildout.

My role was connected to supporting the sales process, helping position the solution, and making sure the customer understood the value of using solar technology for a crosswalk application. Unlike a standard lighting project, this type of system is tied directly to safety, movement, and how drivers and pedestrians interact in a public space.

For me, this project represents experience with smart transportation infrastructure, direct purchasing, and solar-powered safety solutions. It showed how the right product can solve a real roadway safety need while keeping the purchasing path simple and practical.

For me, this project represents experience with solar-powered public amenities, community infrastructure, and solutions that create value beyond the original product. It showed how solar technology can support everyday public spaces while also helping institutions modernize the way they serve their community.

For me, this project represents experience with transit-focused solar lighting, public-sector infrastructure, and solutions that improve the rider experience in a simple but meaningful way. This project involved working with ET&T distributors on solar bollards for apartment communities. The opportunity focused on using solar-powered lighting to improve visibility, safety, and the overall look of residential outdoor spaces.

My role was connected to supporting the distributor relationship and helping position solar bollards as a practical solution for apartment properties. For multifamily communities, lighting matters because it affects how residents, guests, and property managers experience walkways, entrances, parking areas, and shared outdoor spaces.

For me, this project represents experience supporting distributor-led opportunities, residential infrastructure needs, and solar lighting solutions outside of the traditional municipal market. It showed how the same solar technology used in public spaces can also create value in private communities.

name: Naples
description: This project involved a solar lighting pilot for Collier County that was won through a strong understanding of cooperative contracts. The opportunity focused on helping the county evaluate solar-powered lighting as a pra
backstory:
This project involved a solar lighting pilot for Collier County that was won through a strong understanding of cooperative contracts. The opportunity focused on helping the county evaluate solar-powered lighting as a practical way to improve visibility, safety, and public-space functionality without requiring a full traditional electrical buildout.

My role was connected to understanding the purchasing path and helping position the solution through the right contract vehicle. By knowing how cooperative contracts could be used, we were able to make the process easier for the customer and create a clear path for the pilot to move forward.

For me, this project represents the value of knowing procurement, not just product. It showed how understanding cooperative contracts can help turn interest into action, reduce friction for public-sector buyers, and create a real win from a pilot opportunity.
